test_id_1.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_1.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from February 1, 2024, and should therefore receive more weight regarding guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims. The provider manual specifies that Medicaid enrolled pharmacies must submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligible members, with payment not exceeding the Medicaid-only fee, while policy documents primarily refer to general pharmacy billing guidelines or specific immunization services for dual eligibles.", "policy_definition": "Policy documents indicate that for dual eligible members, pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for immunization services, with dual eligible members continuing to access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021]. For general pharmacy claims, providers are directed to refer to the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — April 01, 2023]. Policy also states that pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims as outlined in the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines and Medicaid Update articles, requiring NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor) to be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — February 01, 2024].", "provider_manual_definition": "Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are federally required to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 01, 2025]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider for these services will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the combined Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources implicitly acknowledge the existence of dual eligible members and the interaction between Medicare and Medicaid for their services [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly details the federal requirement for Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligible members, and outlines payment limitations (not exceeding Medicaid-only fee) and the prohibition against billing for Medicare Part B deductibles/coinsurance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — October 01, 2025].", "Policy documents, particularly the August 2021 update, specifically address immunization services for dual eligible members, stating that they continue to access these services through Medicare, and pharmacies can only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for immunizations [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021].", "Policy documents frequently refer providers to other comprehensive billing guidelines or policy manuals for general pharmacy claims or specific drug programs like 340B, rather than providing direct guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims within the update articles themselves [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — April 01, 2023], [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — February 01, 2024]."]}

test_id_2.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_2.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy guidance from February 1, 2024, and should therefore receive more weight regarding dual eligible pharmacy claims. Both sources emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort for dual eligible members, requiring Medicare to be billed first.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source dated February 1, 2023, Medicaid is the payer of last resort for dual eligible enrollees, meaning all other reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid, as per federal law [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 - February 1, 2023]. Earlier policy guidance from August 1, 2021, stated that pharmacies could only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for immunization services, with dual eligible members accessing these services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 - August 1, 2021].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are federally required to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of their CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 - October 1, 2025]. The NYS Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies to ensure dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and state laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 - October 1, 2025]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 - October 1, 2025]. Medicare and Medicaid payments must be accepted as payment in full, and providers cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 - October 1, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources indicate that Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort for dual eligible members, requiring Medicare to be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 - February 1, 2023] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 - October 1, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly states that Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 - October 1, 2025], a detail not present in the provided policy excerpts.", "The provider manual specifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee, and no additional payment will be made if Medicare payment is higher, with both payments accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 - October 1, 2025]. This level of detail regarding payment caps and acceptance as payment in full is not found in the provided policy documents.", "An older policy document from August 1, 2021, specifically stated that pharmacies could only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for immunization services, with dual eligible members accessing these services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 - August 1, 2021], which is a specific service-related constraint not reiterated in the newer policy or the provider manual excerpts."]}

test_id_3.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_3.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, provides more recent and detailed guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims compared to the latest policy updates from April 2023. The provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source dated April 1, 2023, for pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy and page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — April 1, 2023]. Earlier policy guidance from February 1, 2023, states that Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring potential third-party reimbursement sources, including Medicare, to be billed prior to Medicaid for dual eligible enrollees [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 1, 2023]. For immunization services, dual eligible members continue to access services through Medicare, and pharmacies can only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 1, 2021].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are federally required to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 1, 2025]. The NYS Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — October 1, 2025]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025]. Pharmacy providers cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources indicate that Medicare is the primary payer for dual eligible members, with Medicaid acting as the payer of last resort [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 1, 2023] and requiring pharmacies to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 1, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly states that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and that no additional payment will be made if Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, a detail not specified in the provided policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025].", "The provider manual details the requirement for Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to serve dual eligible members, including different types of Medicare enrollment (CMS-460, CMS-855B, CMS-855S), which is not elaborated upon in the provided policy excerpts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 1, 2025].", "Policy documents specifically mention that pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for immunization services, with dual eligible members accessing these services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 1, 2021], a specific service carve-out not present in the provided provider manual excerpts."]}

test_id_6.query: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_6.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims
test_id_6.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from April 1, 2025, and provides more detailed guidance on primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, specifically stating that Medicare benefits must be maximized before billing Medicaid. Both sources emphasize the provider's responsibility to ensure proper billing to the primary payer.", "policy_definition": "Policy documents indicate that certain services may be rejected by the primary payer, but coverage should still be pursued, including situations where prior authorization is needed from the primary payer or when billing Medicare Part D for services covered by Part B, or vice versa, and receiving a rejection [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:8 — 2025-04-01]. It is the responsibility of providers, including pharmacies, to actively and regularly seek Third Party Liability (TPL) enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients, and to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as a commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01]. Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs excluded from TPL, including Medicare, through coordination of benefits [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01]. For pharmacy claims, providers are directed to refer to the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy and the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, Medicare benefits must be maximized prior to billing Medicaid for services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full, and a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. It is the provider's responsibility to determine if the patient is covered by other insurance and if that insurance covers the billed service [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].", "similarities": ["Both sources emphasize the provider's responsibility to ensure proper billing to the primary payer [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].", "Both sources acknowledge that Medicaid acts as a secondary payer, covering patient responsibility or allowed amounts after the primary payer [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly states that Medicare benefits must be maximized prior to billing Medicaid for services covered by both [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01], a detail not as directly stated in the provided policy excerpts.", "The provider manual specifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only rate and that Medicare/Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full, prohibiting recovery of Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not contain these specific payment limitations.", "The policy documents provide examples of situations where coverage should still be pursued even after primary payer rejection, such as needing prior authorization or incorrect billing between Medicare Part B and D [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:8 — 2025-04-01], which are not detailed in the provided provider manual excerpts."]}

test_id_7.query: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_7.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims
test_id_7.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from April 1, 2025, and provides more detailed guidance on primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims. Both sources agree that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and other insurance, including Medicare, must be billed first.", "policy_definition": "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, meaning all other available resources, including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid for dual-eligible enrollees [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. This federal requirement ensures that Medicaid only makes payments after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. Providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "For dual-eligible members, enrollment in a Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries items that would be billed to Medicare on behalf of the member [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:23 — 2025-10-01]. test_id_14.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, which is effective May 1, 2024. Both sources indicate that providers cannot recover co-payments directly from dual-eligible individuals, but the provider manual also details how pharmacies should handle co-payments for Medicaid-only patients, which is not addressed in the policy documents provided.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source dated May 1, 2024, a provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare payment [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 1, 2024]. Earlier policy guidance from October 1, 2019, also reminded providers to accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid claims and noted that inflating patient responsibility amounts could lead to overpayment by Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update October 2019 Volume 35 Number 10:5, 6 — October 1, 2019]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025]. For Medicaid-only patients, providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the copayment each time services are provided or sending bills. However, pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment collected from a Medicaid member, as each claim requiring a co-payment will have it automatically deducted from the final payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — October 1, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both the policy documents and the provider manual state that providers cannot recover co-payment or co-insurance amounts directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 1, 2024]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual specifies that for Medicaid-only patients, providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through various legal means, but must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the collected copayment, as it will be automatically deducted [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — October 1, 2025]. The policy documents do not provide specific guidance on co-payment collection methods for Medicaid-only patients.", "The policy documents explicitly mention Medicare Part C benefits and the responsibility of providers to report Medicare Advantage Plan deductible, co-insurance, co-payment, and paid amounts on claims [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 1, 2024]. test_id_15.query: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?
test_id_15.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from May 1, 2024, and provides more detailed guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members, particularly concerning pharmacy services. The provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source dated May 1, 2024, a provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare payment as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 1, 2024]. Earlier policy guidance from February 1, 2023, states that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and other reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed prior to Medicaid [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 1, 2023].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full, and the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the amount the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025]. For Medicare Part D members, Medicaid only pays for drugs specifically excluded by law from Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 1, 2025]. Providers may attempt to collect outstanding co-payments through legal means, such as requesting payment or sending bills, but must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the collected co-payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — October 1, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources indicate that providers are generally required to accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full for dual-eligible individuals, prohibiting the recovery of certain cost-sharing amounts directly from the member. The policy states this for Medicare Part C benefits [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 1, 2024], while the provider manual specifies it for Medicare Part B benefits for pharmacy providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025].", "Both sources imply that Medicaid acts as a secondary payer to Medicare for dual-eligible members, with policy explicitly stating Medicaid is the payer of last resort [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 1, 2023] and the provider manual detailing that Medicaid only pays for specific drugs not covered by Medicare Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 1, 2025]."], "differences": ["The policy guidance specifically addresses co-payment responsibility for Medicare Part C benefits, stating providers cannot recover co-payment or co-insurance amounts directly from dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 1, 2024]. In contrast, the provider manual focuses on pharmacy providers and Medicare Part B benefits, prohibiting them from seeking to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025].", "The provider manual provides more detailed instructions regarding the collection of co-payments, stating that providers may attempt to collect outstanding co-payments through legal methods but must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the collected co-payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — October 1, 2025]. Policy documents do not provide this specific detail regarding Part D coverage for dual-eligible members."]}

This document also states that failure to submit the claim to the third party will result in denial by Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under Medicare Part D plans [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]. Examples of such excluded drugs include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]. It is generally not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless it falls under these specific exclusions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025].", "similarities": [], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly identifies specific categories of drugs (e.g., select prescription vitamins, certain non-prescription drugs) that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage but are reimbursable by Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]. In contrast, the policy documents generally state that if a primary insurer (like Medicare Part D) does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim, without detailing specific exceptions for Part D-excluded drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]."]}

test_id_21.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.

Both sources agree that pharmacies must bill Medicare before Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payor of last resort.", "policy_definition": "New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — May 01, 2024]. It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, including Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020].", "provider_manual_definition": "Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program, and Medicare benefits must be maximized prior to billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. Providers are responsible for determining if a patient is covered by other insurance and if that insurance covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18 — Jul 23, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both the policy documents and the provider manual state that Medicaid is the payor of last resort and that other applicable third parties, including Medicare, must be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — May 01, 2024]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources emphasize the pharmacy's responsibility in ensuring proper billing to the correct party before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18 — Jul 23, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly states that pharmacies enrolled in Medicaid must demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program, a detail not found in the provided policy excerpts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].", "The provider manual specifically mentions that services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare, providing a more direct instruction than the general 'bill applicable third parties' found in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]."]}

Both sources agree that pharmacies must bill Medicare before Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.", "policy_definition": "New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — 2024-05-01]. It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, including Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full, and a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both the policy documents and the provider manual state that pharmacies must bill Medicare before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — 2024-05-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources indicate that Medicaid is considered the 'payer of last resort' [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — 2024-05-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources require pharmacies enrolled as Medicaid billing providers to also participate in the Medicare Program [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:5 — 2019-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual specifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and that no additional payment will be made if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, a detail not explicitly covered in the provided policy excerpts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].", "The provider manual explicitly states that Medicare and Medicaid payments must be accepted as payment in full, and providers cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from Medicaid members, which is not detailed in the policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]."]}

test_id_26.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the policy documents, which have a most recent effective date of April 1, 2023. The provider manual indicates that failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility to pursue other coverage before billing Medicaid, while policy documents emphasize resolving prior authorization requirements with the third party prior to submitting to Medicaid.", "policy_definition": "For crossover claims, prior authorization (PA) requirements must be resolved with the third party prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. Medicaid will not cover a claim if the primary insurer does not cover a medication [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. Providers are required to first bill Medicare or other third-party insurance for services before submitting a claim to Medicaid to ensure Medicaid is the payor of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01]. Some items, such as Long-Acting Reversible Contraceptives (LARC) or other birth control methods, devices, or supplies covered by NYS Medicaid FFS, do not require prior approval [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:6, 7 — 2023-04-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, and Medicaid regulations require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. If there is a responsible third party, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility to pursue other coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01]. If a prescription/order requires prior approval, the prior approval/authorization number must be entered when billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — 2025-07-23].", "similarities": ["Both sources emphasize that Medicaid is the payor of last resort and requires providers to pursue third-party coverage first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources acknowledge the existence and necessity of prior authorization for certain claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — 2025-07-23]."], "differences": ["The policy documents state that prior authorization requirements must be resolved with the third party prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. test_id_27.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the policy documents, which have a newest effective date of March 1, 2021. Both sources indicate that prior authorization requirements must be addressed with the primary insurer before billing Medicaid, but neither explicitly details requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims.", "policy_definition": "Policy documents state that providers must first bill Medicare or other third-party insurance for services before submitting a claim to Medicaid to ensure Medicaid is the payor of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — October 1, 2020]. Any claim issues, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, must be resolved with the third party prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 1, 2020]. Failure to obtain prior authorization from the third party does not overcome this responsibility [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025]. The manual also specifies that if a prescription/order requires prior approval or prior authorization, the assigned number should be entered in Field 12 of the billing form [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — July 23, 2025]. Prior Authorization (PA) is required for all topical compounded drug products on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:36, 37 — October 1, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources emphasize that Medicaid is the payor of last resort and requires providers to bill third-party insurers (like Medicare) first before submitting claims to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — October 1, 2020]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025].", "Both sources indicate that prior authorization requirements must be addressed with the primary insurer before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 1, 2020]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly states that failure to obtain prior authorization from the third party does not overcome the responsibility to bill them first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025], a detail not explicitly covered in the provided policy excerpts.", "The policy documents mention that if the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 1, 2020], which is not directly addressed in the provided provider manual excerpts.", "The provider manual provides specific instructions for entering prior approval/authorization numbers on billing forms (Field 12) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — July 23, 2025], which is not detailed in the policy documents."]}

test_id_31.query: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?
test_id_31.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, which has an effective date of April 1, 2025. Both sources consistently state that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring all other available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment, with the provider manual offering more specific guidance on Workers' Compensation cases.", "policy_definition": "Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first, with NYS Medicaid always acting as the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be utilized before NYS Medicaid considers payment. If a responsible third-party, such as a health insurance provider, should pay for a patient's health benefits, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 1, 2025]. NYS Medicaid then pays the lesser of the Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — October 1, 2022].", "provider_manual_definition": "Coordination of Benefits (COB) requires that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, as mandated by federal regulations. Providers are required to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025]. If a member has a work-related injury with an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. However, if a drug claim is not related to a Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources agree that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 1, 2025] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025].", "Both sources state that federal regulations require all other available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 1, 2025] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual provides specific guidance regarding Workers’ Compensation cases, stating that Workers’ Compensation coverage must be used first for work-related injuries, but NYRx can be billed as primary for unrelated conditions with a specific Eligibility Clarification Code [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025]. The policy documents do not detail this specific scenario.", "The policy document from October 2022 specifies that NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — October 1, 2022], which is not explicitly mentioned in the provided provider manual excerpts."]}

test_id_33.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document dated April 1, 2025, and should therefore receive more weight. Both sources agree that New York State Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires providers to bill other liable third parties first.", "policy_definition": "New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first, meaning if there is a responsible third-party health insurance provider, they should pay first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6, 7 — 2022-10-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, and Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. If a responsible third party should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as the primary payor, that third party must pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. For work-related injuries with an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. However, if a drug claim is not related to a Workers’ Compensation case, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources state that New York State Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources require providers to bill applicable third parties or utilize other available resources before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual specifically mentions Workers’ Compensation as a third-party resource that must be utilized prior to billing NYRx Medicaid for work-related injuries, while also providing an exception for non-work-related claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not detail specific types of third-party liability beyond general health insurance providers."]}

test_id_34.answer: {"headline_summary": "The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides the most current and detailed requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB), emphasizing that Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. While the newest policy update from April 1, 2025, reiterates this principle, the provider manual offers more specific scenarios and exceptions, and should receive more weight for current guidance.", "policy_definition": "New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01]. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be utilized before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01]. Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — 2022-10-01]. Providers must accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication of the claim as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB) when submitting COB claims to NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, and Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) mandate providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. If there is a responsible third party, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not negate the responsibility to coordinate benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. For work-related injuries with an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. However, if a drug claim is not related to a Workers’ Compensation case, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources agree that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort and that federal regulations require all other available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources state that providers are required to bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual provides specific scenarios for COB, such as requirements for Workers’ Compensation cases and exceptions for unrelated drug claims, which are not detailed in the policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].", "The provider manual explicitly states that failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility to coordinate benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], a point not directly addressed in the provided policy excerpts.", "Policy documents mention the requirement for COB claims to accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB) [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01], which is not explicitly detailed in the provided provider manual excerpts."]}

test_id_35.query: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?
test_id_35.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, which has an effective date of April 1, 2025. Both sources consistently state that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring all other available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment, with the provider manual offering more specific guidance on Workers' Compensation cases.", "policy_definition": "Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first, with NYS Medicaid always acting as the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources must be utilized before NYS Medicaid considers payment. If a responsible third-party, such as a health insurance provider, should pay for a patient's health benefits, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 1, 2025]. NYS Medicaid then pays the lesser of the Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — October 1, 2022]. Providers are required to bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 1, 2025].", "provider_manual_definition": "Coordination of Benefits (COB) requires that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, as mandated by federal regulations. Providers are required by Medicaid regulations (18NYCRR §540.6(e)) to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025]. If a responsible third party is the primary payer for a Medicaid member's health benefits, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025]. For Workers’ Compensation cases, if a member has an open case for a work-related injury, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be used before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. However, if a drug claim is not related to a Workers’ Compensation case (e.g., maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition), NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources agree that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 1, 2025] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025].", "Both sources state that federal regulations require all other available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 1, 2025] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025].", "Both sources emphasize that if a responsible third-party should pay, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 1, 2025] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 1, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual provides specific guidance regarding Workers’ Compensation cases, detailing when Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized and when NYRx may be billed as the primary payer for unrelated conditions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025], which is not explicitly detailed in the provided policy documents.", "The policy documents mention that NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — October 1, 2022], a detail not present in the provided provider manual excerpts."]}

test_id_36.query: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_36.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing
test_id_36.answer: {"headline_summary": "The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual (effective 2025-10-01) is more recent than the New York State Medicaid Update policy documents (newest effective 2023-07-01) and provides more specific guidance on pharmacists' responsibilities when patient or prescriber information is missing. The provider manual indicates pharmacists are not required to enter missing patient information if it's readily available or the prescription is deemed legitimate, and they must make a good faith effort to verify unknown prescribers. Policy documents primarily address notifying prescribers for prior authorization of non-preferred drugs.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source, the New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 (effective 2023-07-01), when presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires prior authorization (PA), the pharmacist must notify the prescriber. PA requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or an authorized agent of the prescriber [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01]. Older policy documents state that pharmacy owners and supervising pharmacists have a responsibility to ensure recognized standards of pharmacy practice are maintained [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — 2023-04-01] and that the supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:9 — 2023-05-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source, the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines (effective 2025-10-01), if the address, age, or CIN of a Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter these items on the prescription if the information is readily available in the pharmacy's records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]. test_id_41.query: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_41.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status
test_id_41.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, and the newest policy source dated January 1, 2025, weight loss drugs are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid. The provider manual is more recent and should receive more weight by default for current guidance.", "policy_definition": "NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for weight loss. Specifically, indications for weight loss are not covered pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 - 2025-01-01]. This exclusion also applies to agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain, as per New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 - 2022-04-01]. Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 - 2024-04-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are examples of drugs/drug uses that are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 - 2025-10-01]. Additionally, compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 - 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both the policy documents and the provider manual explicitly state that drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 - 2025-01-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 - 2025-10-01].", "Both sources cite the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as a basis for the exclusion of drugs for weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 - 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 - 2025-10-01].", "Both sources mention that compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 - 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 - 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The policy documents specifically reference New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3) as an additional basis for excluding agents used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 - 2022-04-01], which is not explicitly mentioned in the provided provider manual excerpts.", "The policy documents provide an example of a specific drug, Wegovy, and explicitly state its exclusion for weight loss indications [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 - 2025-01-01], whereas the provider manual excerpts do not list specific drug examples for this exclusion."]}

For claims submitted to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, 340B-purchased drugs must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — June 1, 2023]. Incorrect or missing claim level identifiers can lead to the covered entity being responsible for reimbursing manufacturers for 340B discounts if rebates are collected by the NYS Department of Health [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — November 1, 2021].", "provider_manual_definition": "340B purchased drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost, plus a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — October 1, 2025]. Claims for 340B drugs submitted in NCPDP format require specific fields, including Submission Clarification Code (SCC) '20', Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) '08' for Medicaid Primary Claims, and Ingredient Cost Submitted as '340B Acquisition Cost' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — October 1, 2025]. For FFS-only claims, the UD modifier must be appended to the 340B drug line [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 - 2022-07-01]. System editing compares the submitted ingredient cost with the 340B ceiling price for Medicaid FFS primary claims [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 - 2021-11-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, 340B purchased drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost, along with a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 - 2025-10-01]. test_id_51.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, which has an effective date of January 1, 2022. The provider manual clarifies that home infusion drugs are not covered by a bundled payment and must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit, while policy documents discuss general drug administration coverage and pharmacy billing responsibilities.", "policy_definition": "New York State Medicaid policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods when provided for FDA-approved indications and recognized off-label indications listed in drug compendia [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — February 01, 2019]. Payment for drug administration in a clinic setting is made through Ambulatory Patient Groups (APG) and for inpatient administration through All Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) [New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11:5 — November 01, 2019]. It is the pharmacy's responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020]. For Fee-for-Service (FFS) pharmacy coverage and policy questions, providers should contact the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy Policy Unit [New York State Medicaid Update January 2022 Volume 38 Number 1:13 — January 01, 2022].", "provider_manual_definition": "The New York State Medicaid Program does not offer a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, which include drugs, supplies, and services. Instead, home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — October 01, 2025]. Drugs, including home infusion drugs, that require administration by a practitioner can be covered under both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit, provided they meet specific criteria such as being billable in the NCPDP format [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — October 01, 2025]. Providers are responsible for determining if a patient has other insurance coverage and if that carrier covers the billed service [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — July 23, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources indicate that drugs requiring administration by a healthcare provider, including home infusion drugs, are covered by Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — February 01, 2019], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — October 01, 2025].", "Both sources emphasize the provider's responsibility in billing, with policy stating the pharmacy's responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020] and the provider manual stating the provider's responsibility to determine other insurance coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — July 23, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly states that home infusion treatments are not covered by a bundled payment and must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — October 01, 2025], a detail not specified in the policy documents.", "Policy documents discuss payment mechanisms for drug administration in clinic or inpatient settings via APG or APR-DRG [New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11:5 — November 01, 2019], while the provider manual focuses on the billing of home infusion drugs as pharmacy or medical benefits and the criteria for pharmacy benefit coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — October 01, 2025], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — October 01, 2025]."]}

test_id_53.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document (December 1, 2024) and provides more detailed guidance on home infusion drug coverage and billing responsibilities. According to the provider manual, home infusion drugs and supplies are not covered by a bundled payment and must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit, with the provider responsible for determining other insurance coverage. Policy documents indicate that pharmacies are responsible for timely billing to the appropriate party.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source dated December 1, 2024, questions regarding NYS Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) drug coverage and policy should be directed to the Office of Health Insurance Programs Division of Program Development and Management [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]. An earlier policy update from July 2020 states that it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]. Drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods are covered when provided for FDA-approved indications and recognized off-label indications listed in drug compendia [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — Feb 01, 2019].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, the New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, meaning home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025]. Certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider can be covered under both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit, provided they meet criteria such as being billable in NCPDP format [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025]. The provider is responsible for determining if the patient has other insurance coverage and if that carrier covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — Jul 23, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources indicate that drugs, including home infusion drugs, are covered under Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — Feb 01, 2019], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources imply the need for providers to understand billing responsibilities and appropriate parties for claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — Jul 23, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly states that there is no bundled payment for home infusion treatments and that drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], a detail not present in the policy documents.", "The provider manual specifies that the provider is responsible for determining other insurance coverage for the patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — Jul 23, 2025], while policy documents state it is the pharmacy's responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party (e.g., commercial plan, Medicare, Medicaid) [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]."]}

According to the provider manual, home infusion drugs and supplies are not covered by a bundled payment and must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit, with the provider responsible for determining other insurance coverage. Policy documents indicate that pharmacies are responsible for timely billing to the appropriate party.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source dated December 1, 2024, questions regarding NYS Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) drug coverage and policy should be directed to the Office of Health Insurance Programs Division of Program Development and Management, and FFS billing and claim questions to the eMedNY Call Center [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]. Earlier policy guidance from July 2020 states that it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]. Home infusion drugs requiring administration by a health care provider can be covered under both medical and pharmacy benefits, provided they meet criteria such as being billable in NCPDP format and having a National Drug Code (NDC) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025]. test_id_56.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from February 1, 2024, and provides more detailed information on the general reimbursement process for drugs, including how pharmacies are reimbursed for medications. Policy documents specifically address naloxone coverage, including over-the-counter (OTC) naloxone, while the provider manual outlines general drug reimbursement principles.", "policy_definition": "New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by a practitioner for a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — February 1, 2024]. Medicaid policy also requires Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) to provide at least five days’ coverage for emergencies, without prior authorization, for medications used to treat substance use disorders, which would include naloxone [New York State Medicaid Update May 2020 Volume 36 Number 10:7, 8 — May 1, 2020].", "provider_manual_definition": "The NYRx provides reimbursement for prescription drugs included on the NY Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — October 1, 2025]. Pharmacies are reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using the established reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 1, 2025]. Reimbursement is only available for multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:33 — October 1, 2025]. Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, unless provided by a facility that includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 1, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources indicate that reimbursement is tied to a list of approved drugs. The policy documents imply coverage for naloxone [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — February 1, 2024], while the provider manual explicitly states reimbursement for drugs on the NY Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — October 1, 2025]."], "differences": ["Policy documents specifically address naloxone, including its coverage when prescribed OTC or dispensed under a standing order [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — February 1, 2024], and mention emergency coverage for substance use disorder medications without prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Update May 2020 Volume 36 Number 10:7, 8 — May 1, 2020]. The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides general guidelines for drug reimbursement, focusing on billing via NDC and the requirement for drugs to be on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, without specific mention of naloxone or OTC coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15, 31, 33 — October 1, 2025]."]}

test_id_60.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, which is dated February 1, 2024. Therefore, the provider manual should receive more weight regarding how naloxone is reimbursed. Both sources indicate that naloxone is covered by Medicaid, with the provider manual detailing general reimbursement for prescription drugs and the policy specifically addressing over-the-counter naloxone coverage.", "policy_definition": "New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by a practitioner for a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — February 1, 2024]. Medicaid policy also requires Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) to provide at least five days’ coverage for emergencies, without prior authorization, for medications used to treat substance use disorders, which includes medication associated with management [New York State Medicaid Update May 2020 Volume 36 Number 10:7, 8 — May 1, 2020].", "provider_manual_definition": "Reimbursement for prescription drugs, including naloxone, is available for those included on the NY Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — October 1, 2025]. Prescriptions must be dispensed and billed by a Medicaid enrolled pharmacy, using the member’s individual Medicaid Client Identification Number (CIN) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — October 1, 2025]. When billing for the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field, and the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 1, 2025]. If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 1, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources indicate that naloxone is covered by New York State Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — February 1, 2024] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — October 1, 2025]."], "differences": ["The policy documents specifically mention coverage for over-the-counter (OTC) naloxone when prescribed or dispensed under a standing order [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — February 1, 2024], while the provider manual focuses on general reimbursement procedures for prescription drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — October 1, 2025].", "The provider manual details the specific billing requirements for reimbursement, including the use of the member's CIN, prescriber's NPI, and NDC for the drug, and separate billing for administration [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15, 31 — October 1, 2025]. The policy documents do not provide these granular billing instructions.", "Policy documents mention that Medicaid policy requires at least five days’ coverage for emergencies for medications used to treat substance use disorders, without prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Update May 2020 Volume 36 Number 10:7, 8 — May 1, 2020], a detail not present in the provided provider manual excerpts."]}
